does: This paper introduces Backdoor Attribution (BkdAttr), a causal analysis framework for understanding and mitigating backdoor attacks in fine-tuned language models. The authors develop the Backdoor Probe to demonstrate that backdoor features are learnable and encoded in model representations, then present Backdoor Attention Head Attribution (BAHA) to identify specific attention heads responsible for processing these features. Through empirical investigation, they show that backdoor functionality is sparse—ablating approximately 3% of attention heads reduces Attack Success Rate by over 90%, demonstrating both the interpretability and vulnerability of backdoor mechanisms in LLMs.
connects: This work extends the growing interpretability literature on LLM safety and trustworthiness, building on prior work examining alignment, jailbreak vulnerabilities, and hallucinations in large models. It responds directly to the gap in mechanistic understanding of data-poisoning backdoor attacks specifically. The causal analysis approach builds on established interpretability techniques like attention attribution and feature probing from mechanistic interpretability research, while connecting to work on understanding how harmful behaviors encode in neural networks through poisoned training data.
enables: This research provides a foundation for more targeted and efficient backdoor defenses by identifying which model components are critical for attack success. The sparse nature of backdoor-responsible heads suggests practical defense strategies beyond full model retraining. Future work could leverage these insights to develop real-time detection mechanisms, design architectural modifications to prevent backdoor feature encoding, or create robust training procedures that prevent poisoning from taking hold in specific attention components, advancing the broader goal of securing fine-tuned LLMs against supply-chain threats.
